Labour sits on the [centre-left](/wiki/Centre-left_politics "Centre-left politics") of the political spectrum.[[175]](#cite_note-175) It was formed to provide political representation for the [trade union movement](/wiki/Trade_union_movement "Trade union movement") in Parliament. The Labour Party gained a socialist commitment with the party constitution of 1918, [Clause IV](/wiki/Clause_IV "Clause IV") of which called for the "common ownership", or [nationalisation](/wiki/Nationalisation "Nationalisation"), of the "means of production, distribution and exchange". Although about a third of British industry was taken into public ownership after the Second World War and remained so until the 1980s, the right of the party was questioning the validity of expanding on this by the late 1950s. Influenced by [Anthony Crosland](/wiki/Anthony_Crosland "Anthony Crosland")'s book *[The Future of Socialism](/wiki/The_Future_of_Socialism "The Future of Socialism")* (1956), the circle around party leader [Hugh Gaitskell](/wiki/Hugh_Gaitskell "Hugh Gaitskell") felt that the commitment was no longer necessary. An attempt to remove Clause IV from the party constitution in 1959 failed; Tony Blair and New Labour "modernisers" were successful in removing Clause IV in 1994.[[176]](#cite_note-historytoday.com-176)[[177]](#cite_note-177)[[178]](#cite_note-independent.co.uk-178)

Historically influenced by [Keynesian economics](/wiki/Keynesian_economics "Keynesian economics"), the party favoured [government intervention](/wiki/Government_intervention "Government intervention") in the economy and the [redistribution](/wiki/Income_redistribution "Income redistribution") of wealth. Taxation was seen as a means to achieve a "major redistribution of wealth and income" in the October 1974 election manifesto.[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELund2006111-179) The party also desired increased rights for workers and a [welfare state](/wiki/Welfare_state "Welfare state"), including publicly funded healthcare. From the late-1980s onwards, the party adopted [free market](/wiki/Free_market "Free market") policies,[[180]](#cite_note-mulholland1-180) leading many observers to describe the Labour Party as [social democratic](/wiki/Social_democratic "Social democratic") or the [Third Way](/wiki/Third_Way "Third Way"), rather than democratic socialist.[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHay2002114–115HopkinWincott2006Jessop2004McAnulla2006118,_127,_133,_141MerkelPetringHenkesEgle20084,_25–26,_40,_66-181) Other commentators go further and argue that traditional social democratic parties across Europe, including the British Labour Party, have been so deeply transformed in recent years that it is no longer possible to describe them ideologically as "social democratic",[[182]](#cite_note-Lavelle_2008-182) and that this ideological shift has put new strains on the Labour Party's traditional relationship with the trade unions.[[183]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDanielsMcIlroy2009McIlroy2011Smith2009SmithMorton2006-183) Within the party, differentiation was made between the social democratic and the [socialist](/wiki/Socialist "Socialist") wings of the party, the latter often subscribed to a radical socialist, even [Marxist](/wiki/Marxist "Marxist"), ideology.[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrines2011161-184)[[185]](#cite_note-What's_left_of_the_Labour_left-185)

While affirming a commitment to [democratic socialism](/wiki/Democratic_socialism "Democratic socialism"),[[186]](#cite_note-constitution-186)[[187]](#cite_note-hww-187) the new version of Clause IV no longer definitely commits the party to public ownership of industry and in its place advocates "the enterprise of the market and the rigour of competition" along with "high quality public services [...] either owned by the public or accountable to them".[[186]](#cite_note-constitution-186) MPs in the [Socialist Campaign Group](/wiki/Socialist_Campaign_Group "Socialist Campaign Group") and the [Labour Representation Committee](/wiki/Labour_Representation_Committee_(2004) "Labour Representation Committee (2004)") see themselves as standard bearers for the radical socialist tradition in contrast to the democratic socialist tradition represented by organisations such as [Compass](/wiki/Compass_(think_tank) "Compass (think tank)") and the magazine *[Tribune](/wiki/Tribune_(magazine) "Tribune (magazine)")*.[[188]](#cite_note-labourlist.org-188) The group [Progress](/wiki/Progress_(organisation) "Progress (organisation)"), founded in 1996, represents the centrist position in the party and was opposed to the Corbyn leadership.[[189]](#cite_note-progress-20170302-189)[[190]](#cite_note-progress-20170720-190) In 2015, [Momentum](/wiki/Momentum_(organisation) "Momentum (organisation)") was created by [Jon Lansman](/wiki/Jon_Lansman "Jon Lansman") as a grass-roots left-wing organisation following [Jeremy Corbyn](/wiki/Jeremy_Corbyn "Jeremy Corbyn")'s election as party leader. Rather than organising among the [PLP](/wiki/Parliamentary_Labour_Party "Parliamentary Labour Party"), Momentum is a rank-and-file grouping with an estimated 40,000 members.[[191]](#cite_note-191) The party also has a [Christian socialist](/wiki/Christian_socialist "Christian socialist") faction, the [Christians on the Left](/wiki/Christians_on_the_Left "Christians on the Left") society.[[192]](#cite_note-192)[[193]](#cite_note-193)[[194]](#cite_note-194)

### Symbols

Labour has long been identified with red, a [political colour](/wiki/Political_colour "Political colour") traditionally affiliated with socialism and the [labour movement](/wiki/Labour_movement "Labour movement"). Prior to the red flag logo, the party had used a modified version of the classic 1924 shovel, torch, and quill emblem. In 1924, a brand-conscious Labour leadership had devised a competition, inviting supporters to design a logo to replace the 'polo mint' like motif that had previously appeared in party literature. The winning entry, emblazoned with the word "Liberty" over a design incorporating a torch, shovel, and quill symbol, was popularised through its sale, in badge form, for a shilling. The party conference in 1931 passed a motion "That this conference adopts Party Colours, which should be uniform throughout the country, colours to be red and gold".[[195]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-195) During the New Labour period, the colour purple was also used, and the party has employed other colours in certain areas according to local tradition.[[196]](#cite_note-196)[[197]](#cite_note-197)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Red_flag.svg/250px-Red_flag.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Red_flag.svg)

The [red flag](/wiki/Red_flag_(politics) "Red flag (politics)"), originally the official flag and symbol of the Labour Party

Since the party's inception, the [red flag](/wiki/Red_flag_(politics) "Red flag (politics)") has been Labour's official symbol; the flag has been associated with socialism and revolution ever since the 1789 [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") and the [revolutions of 1848](/wiki/Revolutions_of_1848 "Revolutions of 1848"). The [red rose](/wiki/Rose_(symbolism)#Socialism_and_social_democracy "Rose (symbolism)"), a symbol of socialism and social democracy, was adopted as the party symbol in 1986 as part of a rebranding exercise and is now incorporated into the party logo.[[198]](#cite_note-The_Telegraph-198)

The red flag became an inspiration, which resulted in the composition of "[The Red Flag](/wiki/The_Red_Flag "The Red Flag")", the official party anthem since its inception, being sung at the end of party conferences and on various occasions such as in Parliament in February 2006 to mark the centenary of the Labour Party's founding. It still remains in use, although attempts were made to play down the role of the song during New Labour.[[199]](#cite_note-bbc-199)[[200]](#cite_note-Hoggart-200) The song "[Jerusalem](/wiki/And_did_those_feet_in_ancient_time "And did those feet in ancient time")", based on a [William Blake](/wiki/William_Blake "William Blake") poem, is also traditionally sung at the end of party conferences with The Red Flag.[[201]](#cite_note-Telegraph.co.uk-201)[[202]](#cite_note-202)

## Constitution and structure

[Clause IV](/wiki/Clause_IV "Clause IV") (1995)

> The Labour Party is a [democratic socialist](/wiki/Democratic_socialist "Democratic socialist") party. It believes that by the strength of our common endeavour we achieve more than we achieve alone, so as to create for each of us the means to realise our true potential and for all of us a community in which power, wealth and opportunity are in the hands of the many, not the few, where the rights we enjoy reflect the duties we owe, and where we live together, freely, in a spirit of solidarity, tolerance and respect.

Party Constitution, Labour Party Rule Book[[186]](#cite_note-constitution-186)

The Labour Party is a membership organisation consisting of individual members and [constituency Labour parties](/wiki/Constituency_Labour_parties "Constituency Labour parties"), [affiliated trade unions](/wiki/Affiliated_trade_unions "Affiliated trade unions"), [socialist societies](/wiki/Socialist_societies "Socialist societies") and the [Co-operative Party](/wiki/Co-operative_Party "Co-operative Party"), with which it has an electoral agreement. Members who are elected to parliamentary positions take part in the [Parliamentary Labour Party](/wiki/Parliamentary_Labour_Party "Parliamentary Labour Party") (PLP). Prior to [Brexit](/wiki/Brexit "Brexit") in January 2020, members also took part in the [European Parliamentary Labour Party](/wiki/European_Parliamentary_Labour_Party "European Parliamentary Labour Party") (EPLP).

The party's decision-making bodies on a national level formally include the [National Executive Committee](/wiki/National_Executive_Committee_of_the_Labour_Party "National Executive Committee of the Labour Party") (NEC), [Labour Party Conference](/wiki/Labour_Party_Conference "Labour Party Conference") and [National Policy Forum](/wiki/National_Policy_Forum "National Policy Forum") (NPF)—although in practice the Parliamentary leadership has the final say on policy. The 2008 Labour Party Conference was the first at which affiliated trade unions and Constituency Labour Parties did not have the right to submit motions on contemporary issues that would previously have been debated.[[203]](#cite_note-Anger_over_'union_debate_limit'-203) Labour Party conferences now include more "keynote" addresses, guest speakers and question-and-answer sessions, while specific discussion of policy now takes place in the National Policy Forum.

The Labour Party is an [unincorporated association](/wiki/Unincorporated_association "Unincorporated association") without a [separate legal personality](/wiki/Separate_legal_personality "Separate legal personality"), and the Labour Party Rule Book legally regulates the organisation and the relationship with members.[[204]](#cite_note-lgn-20150917-204) The [General Secretary](/wiki/General_Secretary_of_the_Labour_Party "General Secretary of the Labour Party") represents the party on behalf of the other members of the Labour Party in any legal matters or actions.[[205]](#cite_note-205)

### Membership and registered supporters

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Labour_Party_membership_graph.svg/250px-Labour_Party_membership_graph.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Labour_Party_membership_graph.svg)

A graph showing Labour Party individual membership, excluding affiliated members and supporters

As of 31 December 2010, under the new leader [Ed Miliband](/wiki/Ed_Miliband "Ed Miliband"), individual membership of the party was 193,261; a historical low for the Party since the 1930s.[[206]](#cite_note-search-206) Membership remained relatively unchanged in the following years.[[206]](#cite_note-search-206)[[207]](#cite_note-207)[[208]](#cite_note-search2-208) In August 2015, prior to the [2015 leadership election](/wiki/2015_Labour_Party_leadership_election_(UK) "2015 Labour Party leadership election (UK)"), the Labour Party reported 292,505 full members, 147,134 affiliated supporters (mostly from affiliated [trade unions](/wiki/Trade_union "Trade union") and [socialist societies](/wiki/Socialist_societies "Socialist societies")) and 110,827 registered supporters; a total of about 550,000 members and supporters.[[209]](#cite_note-independent-20150910-209)[[210]](#cite_note-mirror-20150825-210)

Following the election of [Jeremy Corbyn](/wiki/Jeremy_Corbyn "Jeremy Corbyn") as leader, individual membership almost doubled to 388,262 in December 2015;[[208]](#cite_note-search2-208) and rose significantly again the following year to 543,645 in December 2016.[[211]](#cite_note-211)
As of December 2017[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Labour_Party_(UK)&action=edit), the party had 564,443 full members,[[212]](#cite_note-labour-201807-212) a peak since 1980 making it the largest political party in Western Europe.[[213]](#cite_note-huffpost-20170613-213)[[214]](#cite_note-214) Consequently, membership fees became the largest component of the party's income, overtaking trade unions donations which were previously of most financial importance, making Labour the most financially well-off British political party in 2017.[[215]](#cite_note-guardian-20180822-215) As of December 2019, the party had 532,046 full members.[[216]](#cite_note-labour-202007-216)

In the [2020 leadership election](/wiki/2020_Labour_Party_leadership_election_(UK) "2020 Labour Party leadership election (UK)"), 490,731 people voted, of which 401,564 (81.8%) were members, 76,161 (15.5%) had affiliated membership and 13,006 (2.6%) were registered supporters. The registered supporter class was abolished in 2021.[[217]](#cite_note-hocl-20220830-217) By December 2023, the party's membership had fallen to 370,450 members.[[218]](#cite_note-bbc-20240822-218) In March 2024, it was revealed the party's membership had reduced further to 366,604 members.[[219]](#cite_note-Membership,_March_2024-219) In January 2025, it was revealed that the party's membership had fallen again, now down to 329,957 members.[[220]](#cite_note-Membership,_January_2025-220) In February 2025, the Labour Party's membership was revealed to be 309,000.[[221]](#cite_note-Membership,_February_2025-221) By 12 December 2025, its membership had fallen to below 250,000, according to internal figures.[[5]](#cite_note-:1-5)

#### Northern Ireland

For many years, Labour held to a policy of not allowing residents of [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland") to apply for membership,[[222]](#cite_note-labour.org.uk-222) instead supporting the [Social Democratic and Labour Party](/wiki/Social_Democratic_and_Labour_Party "Social Democratic and Labour Party") (SDLP) which informally takes the Labour whip in the House of Commons.[[223]](#cite_note-Understanding_Ulster-223) The 2003 Labour Party Conference accepted legal advice that the party could not continue to prohibit residents of the province joining,[[224]](#cite_note-Labour_NI_ban_overturned-224) and while the National Executive has established a regional constituency party it has not yet agreed to contest elections there. In December 2015 a meeting of the members of the Labour Party in Northern Ireland decided unanimously to contest the [elections for the Northern Ireland Assembly held in May 2016](/wiki/2016_Northern_Ireland_Assembly_election "2016 Northern Ireland Assembly election").[[225]](#cite_note-225) The Labour Party in Northern Ireland moved a model motion, in July 2020, for Labour's NEC to allow them a "Right to Stand".[[226]](#cite_note-LabourNI-226) The motion noted how the SDLP's alliance with [Fianna Fáil](/wiki/Fianna_F%C3%A1il "Fianna Fáil"), a member-party of the [Liberal International](/wiki/Liberal_International "Liberal International") in the Republic of Ireland, had meant that it was campaigning against the [Irish Labour Party](/wiki/Irish_Labour_Party "Irish Labour Party"), which it saw as questioning "the legitimacy of Labour's sister party relationship".[[226]](#cite_note-LabourNI-226)

[Unite the Union](/wiki/Unite_the_Union "Unite the Union") showing their support for the Labour party on their [Leeds](/wiki/Leeds "Leeds") offices during the 2015 general election

The [Trade Union and Labour Party Liaison Organisation](/wiki/Trade_Union_and_Labour_Party_Liaison_Organisation "Trade Union and Labour Party Liaison Organisation") is the co-ordinating structure that supports the policy and campaign activities of affiliated union members within the Labour Party at the national, regional and local level.[[227]](#cite_note-ReferenceB-227)

As it was founded by the unions to represent the interests of working-class people, Labour's link with the unions has always been a defining characteristic of the party. In recent years this link has come under increasing strain, with the [RMT](/wiki/National_Union_of_Rail,_Maritime_and_Transport_Workers "National Union of Rail, Maritime and Transport Workers") being expelled from the party in 2004 for allowing its branches in Scotland to affiliate to the left-wing [Scottish Socialist Party](/wiki/Scottish_Socialist_Party "Scottish Socialist Party").[[228]](#cite_note-RMT_'breached'_Labour_party_rules-228) Other unions have also faced calls from members to reduce financial support for the Party[[229]](#cite_note-Labour's_link_to_unions_in_danger-229) and seek more effective political representation for their views on [privatisation](/wiki/Privatisation "Privatisation"), [public spending](/wiki/Public_spending "Public spending") cuts and the anti-[trade union](/wiki/Trade_unions_in_the_United_Kingdom "Trade unions in the United Kingdom") laws.[[230]](#cite_note-TUC_Congress_Voices-230) Unison and [GMB](/wiki/GMB_(trade_union) "GMB (trade union)") have both threatened to withdraw funding from constituency MPs and Dave Prentis of [UNISON](/wiki/UNISON "UNISON") has warned that the union will write "no more blank cheques" and is dissatisfied with "feeding the hand that bites us".[[231]](#cite_note-Dunton-231) Union funding was redesigned in 2013 after the [Falkirk candidate-selection controversy](/wiki/2013_Labour_Party_Falkirk_candidate_selection "2013 Labour Party Falkirk candidate selection").[[232]](#cite_note-BBC_News-232) The [Fire Brigades Union](/wiki/Fire_Brigades_Union "Fire Brigades Union"), which "severed links" with Labour in 2004, re-joined the party under Corbyn's leadership in 2015.[[233]](#cite_note-233)

### European and international affiliation

The Labour Party was a founder member of the [Party of European Socialists](/wiki/Party_of_European_Socialists "Party of European Socialists") (PES). The European Parliamentary Labour Party's 10 [MEPs](/wiki/MEPs "MEPs") were part of the [Socialists and Democrats](/wiki/Socialists_and_Democrats "Socialists and Democrats") (S&D), the second largest [group in the European Parliament](/wiki/Political_groups_of_the_European_Parliament "Political groups of the European Parliament"). The Labour Party was represented by [Emma Reynolds](/wiki/Emma_Reynolds "Emma Reynolds") in the PES presidency.[[234]](#cite_note-ReferenceC-234)

The party was a member of the [Labour and Socialist International](/wiki/Labour_and_Socialist_International "Labour and Socialist International") between 1923 and 1940.[[235]](#cite_note-books.google.com-235) Since 1951, the party has been a member of the [Socialist International](/wiki/Socialist_International "Socialist International"), which was founded thanks to the efforts of the Clement Attlee leadership. In February 2013, the Labour Party NEC decided to downgrade participation to observer membership status, "in view of ethical concerns, and to develop international co-operation through new networks".[[236]](#cite_note-Black-236) Labour was a founding member of the [Progressive Alliance](/wiki/Progressive_Alliance "Progressive Alliance") international founded in co-operation with the [Social Democratic Party of Germany](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_of_Germany "Social Democratic Party of Germany") and other social-democratic parties on 22 May 2013.[[237]](#cite_note-Spiegel.de-237)[[238]](#cite_note-Vorwurf:_SPD_spaltet_die_Linken-238)[[239]](#cite_note-Morgenweb.de-239)[[240]](#cite_note-240)

## Election results

Main article: [Electoral history of the Labour Party (UK)](/wiki/Electoral_history_of_the_Labour_Party_(UK) "Electoral history of the Labour Party (UK)")

For all detailed election results involving the Labour Party including: general elections, devolved national elections, London Assembly, London Mayoral, combined authority and European Parliament elections see: [Electoral history of the Labour Party (UK)](/wiki/Electoral_history_of_the_Labour_Party_(UK) "Electoral history of the Labour Party (UK)").

In all general elections since [1918](/wiki/1918_United_Kingdom_general_election "1918 United Kingdom general election"), Labour has been either the governing party or the [Official Opposition](/wiki/Her_Majesty%27s_Most_Loyal_Opposition_(United_Kingdom) "Her Majesty's Most Loyal Opposition (United Kingdom)").[[241]](#cite_note-:8-241)

### UK general election results

See also: [Elections in the United Kingdom § General elections](/wiki/Elections_in_the_United_Kingdom#General_elections "Elections in the United Kingdom")

Following the [1918 general election](/wiki/1918_United_Kingdom_general_election "1918 United Kingdom general election"), Labour became the Official Opposition after the Conservatives went into [coalition](/wiki/Coalition#Government_and_politics "Coalition") with the [Liberal Party](/wiki/Liberal_Party_(UK) "Liberal Party (UK)").[[241]](#cite_note-:8-241) Labour's first minority governments came following the [1923](/wiki/1923_United_Kingdom_general_election "1923 United Kingdom general election") and [1929](/wiki/1929_United_Kingdom_general_election "1929 United Kingdom general election") general elections, the latter being the first time Labour were the largest party in the country by seats won.[[241]](#cite_note-:8-241) They formed their first majority government following the [1945 general election](/wiki/1945_United_Kingdom_general_election "1945 United Kingdom general election").[[241]](#cite_note-:8-241) However, after winning the [1950 general election](/wiki/1950_United_Kingdom_general_election "1950 United Kingdom general election"), Labour would lose the following election in [1951](/wiki/1951_United_Kingdom_general_election "1951 United Kingdom general election") to the Conservatives despite gaining the highest share of votes to date at 48.8%.[[241]](#cite_note-:8-241) During the [1983](/wiki/1983_United_Kingdom_general_election "1983 United Kingdom general election") election, Labour posted their worst vote share in the post-war period at 27.6%.[[241]](#cite_note-:8-241) In [1997](/wiki/1997_United_Kingdom_general_election "1997 United Kingdom general election"), a party record of 418 Labour MPs were elected.[[241]](#cite_note-:8-241) At the [2024 general election](/wiki/2024_United_Kingdom_general_election "2024 United Kingdom general election"), Labour won a landslide victory and returned to government with [Keir Starmer](/wiki/Keir_Starmer "Keir Starmer") as prime minister.[[161]](#cite_note-:112-161)

See also: [Elections in the United Kingdom § General elections](/wiki/Elections_in_the_United_Kingdom#General_elections "Elections in the United Kingdom")

1. **[^](#cite_ref-249)** The first election held under the [Representation of the People Act 1918](/wiki/Representation_of_the_People_Act_1918 "Representation of the People Act 1918") in which all men over 21, and most women over the age of 30 could vote, and therefore a much larger electorate.[[248]](#cite_note-248)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-260)** First election held under the [Representation of the People (Equal Franchise) Act 1928](/wiki/Representation_of_the_People_(Equal_Franchise)_Act_1928 "Representation of the People (Equal Franchise) Act 1928") which gave all women aged over 21 the vote.[[258]](#cite_note-259)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-268)** Franchise extended to all 18 to 20-year-olds under the [Representation of the People Act 1969](/wiki/Representation_of_the_People_Act_1969 "Representation of the People Act 1969").[[265]](#cite_note-267)
